content.body: An angle is formed when two rays meet at a point called the vertex. Angles are measured in degrees (°). A full rotation is 360°. Understanding angles is essential for geometry, construction, navigation and engineering.

Types of Angles:
Acute angle: less than 90°
Right angle: exactly 90° (shown by a small square)
Obtuse angle: between 90° and 180°
Straight angle: exactly 180°
Reflex angle: between 180° and 360°
Full angle: exactly 360°

content.title: Angle Relationships
content.body:
Complementary angles: two angles that add up to 90°
Example: 35° and 55° are complementary (35+55=90)

Supplementary angles: two angles that add up to 180°
Example: 110° and 70° are supplementary (110+70=180)

Angles on a straight line add up to 180°
Angles at a point add up to 360°
Vertically opposite angles are EQUAL (formed by crossing lines)

content.title: Parallel Lines and Transversals
content.body:
When a line (transversal) crosses two parallel lines:
• Corresponding angles are EQUAL (same position, same side) — F shape
• Alternate angles are EQUAL (Z or N shape)
• Co-interior (same-side) angles add up to 180° (C or U shape)

These rules help solve many geometry problems.

questions.question: An angle of 135° is:
questions.explanation: Obtuse angles are between 90° and 180°. 135° falls in this range.

questions.question: What do angles on a straight line add up to?
questions.explanation: Angles on a straight line always add up to 180° (supplementary).

questions.question: If one angle is 65°, its complementary angle is:
questions.explanation: Complementary angles add to 90°. 90° − 65° = 25°.

questions.question: Vertically opposite angles are:
questions.explanation: Vertically opposite angles (formed when two lines cross) are always equal.

questions.question: Angles at a point add up to:
questions.explanation: All angles around a single point add up to 360°.

questions.question: A reflex angle is:
questions.explanation: Reflex angles are greater than 180° but less than 360°.

questions.question: In parallel lines, alternate angles are:
questions.explanation: Alternate angles (Z-shape) formed by a transversal crossing parallel lines are equal.

questions.question: Co-interior angles in parallel lines add up to:
questions.explanation: Co-interior (same-side interior) angles add up to 180° — they are supplementary.

questions.question: If two angles are supplementary and one is 48°, the other is:
Corresponding angles (F-shape, parallel lines) are:
questions.explanation: Corresponding angles are equal when lines are parallel — they occupy the same position relative to the transversal.

questions.question: What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.question: What is 2x + 3x simplified?
questions.explanation: Collect like terms: 2x + 3x = 5x (both terms have the same variable x).

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.question: What is the result of expanding 4(y - 3)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is 3² equal to?
questions.explanation: 3² = 3 × 3 = 9. The exponent 2 means multiply the base by itself.

questions.question: Factorise: 4x + 8
questions.explanation: The HCF of 4x and 8 is 4. So 4x + 8 = 4(x + 2).

questions.question: What is the y-intercept of y = 3x + 5?
questions.explanation: In y = mx + c, c is the y-intercept. Here c = 5.

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

content.body: A triangle is a polygon with three sides and three angles. The sum of interior angles of ANY triangle is always 180°. Triangles are the most fundamental shape in geometry and appear in architecture, engineering, and art.

Types of Triangles by SIDES:
Equilateral: all 3 sides equal, all angles = 60°
Isosceles: 2 sides equal, 2 base angles equal
Scalene: no sides equal, no angles equal

By ANGLES:
Acute: all angles < 90°
Right-angled: one angle = 90°
Obtuse: one angle > 90°

Area of a Triangle:
Area = ½ × base × height

Perimeter = sum of all three sides

Exterior angle of a triangle = sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles

content.title: Angle Calculations
content.body:
A triangle has angles 50° and 70°. Find the third angle.
Sum of angles = 180°
Third angle = 180° − 50° − 70° = 60°

Exterior angle example:
If interior angles at two vertices are 45° and 65°,
the exterior angle at the third vertex = 45° + 65° = 110°

content.title: Area Calculations
content.body:
Triangle with base = 10 cm, height = 6 cm:
Area = ½ × 10 × 6 = 30 cm²

Equilateral triangle with side = 4 cm:
All angles = 60°, and height = side × (√3/2) = 4 × 0.866 = 3.46 cm
Area = ½ × 4 × 3.46 = 6.93 cm²

questions.question: The sum of angles in any triangle is:
questions.explanation: The interior angles of any triangle always add up to 180°.

questions.question: A triangle has angles 60°, 60°, 60°. It is:
questions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has all sides equal and all angles equal to 60°.

questions.question: Triangle with angles 90°, 45°, 45° is:
questions.explanation: It has a 90° angle (right-angled) and two equal 45° angles (isosceles).

questions.question: Two angles of a triangle are 55° and 75°. Find the third.
Area of a triangle with base 8 cm and height 5 cm:
questions.explanation: Area = ½ × 8 × 5 = 20 cm².

questions.question: An isosceles triangle has one angle of 40° at the apex. Find the base angles.
questions.explanation: Remaining angles = 180° − 40° = 140°. Each base angle = 140°/2 = 70°.

questions.question: The exterior angle of a triangle equals:
options: The sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles
questions.answer: The sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles
questions.explanation: Exterior angle theorem: exterior angle = sum of the two opposite interior angles.

questions.question: Which triangle has NO equal sides?
questions.explanation: A scalene triangle has all three sides of different lengths.

questions.question: Triangle: base=12m, height=7m. Find area.
questions.explanation: Area = ½ × 12 × 7 = 42 m².

questions.question: A triangle has sides 3cm, 4cm, 5cm. What type is it?
questions.explanation: 3²+4²=5² (Pythagoras holds) → right-angled. All sides different → scalene.

questions.question: What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is the y-intercept of y = 3x + 5?

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

content.body: A quadrilateral is a four-sided polygon. The sum of interior angles of any quadrilateral is 360°. Different types of quadrilaterals have special properties that make them useful in design and construction.

Types of Quadrilaterals:
Square: 4 equal sides, 4 right angles, diagonals bisect at 90°
Rectangle: opposite sides equal, 4 right angles
Rhombus: 4 equal sides, opposite angles equal, diagonals bisect at 90°
Parallelogram: opposite sides parallel and equal, opposite angles equal
Trapezium: exactly one pair of parallel sides
Kite: two pairs of adjacent equal sides

Areas of Quadrilaterals:
Square: A = s²
Rectangle: A = l × w
Parallelogram: A = base × height
Trapezium: A = ½(a+b) × h  where a,b are parallel sides, h is height
Rhombus: A = ½ × d₁ × d₂  (d₁,d₂ are diagonals)

content.title: Polygon Angle Sums
content.body:
Sum of interior angles of a polygon with n sides = (n−2) × 180°

Triangle (n=3): (3−2)×180° = 180° ✓
Quadrilateral (n=4): (4−2)×180° = 360° ✓
Pentagon (n=5): (5−2)×180° = 540°
Hexagon (n=6): (6−2)×180° = 720°

Regular polygon: all sides equal, all angles equal
Each interior angle of regular n-gon = (n−2)×180°/n
Regular hexagon: (6−2)×180°/6 = 120°

questions.question: Sum of interior angles of a quadrilateral:
questions.explanation: (4−2)×180° = 360°. All four angles of any quadrilateral add to 360°.

questions.question: A rectangle has length 9m and width 4m. Find area.
questions.explanation: Area = l × w = 9 × 4 = 36 m².

questions.question: What is special about a rhombus?
questions.explanation: A rhombus has all four sides equal in length (like a pushed square).

questions.question: Trapezium has parallel sides 6cm and 10cm, height 4cm. Area = ?
Sum of interior angles of a hexagon:
Each interior angle of a regular pentagon is:
A parallelogram has base 7cm and height 5cm. Area = ?
questions.explanation: Area of parallelogram = base × height = 7 × 5 = 35 cm².

questions.question: Which quadrilateral has exactly ONE pair of parallel sides?
questions.explanation: A trapezium has exactly one pair of parallel sides. A parallelogram has two.

questions.question: A square with side 6cm. Find perimeter.
questions.explanation: Perimeter of square = 4 × side = 4 × 6 = 24 cm.

questions.question: Each interior angle of a regular hexagon is:
What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.explanation: Collect like terms: 2x + 3x = 5x (both terms have the same variable x).

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.question: What is the result of expanding 4(y - 3)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is 3² equal to?

questions.explanation: The HCF of 4x and 8 is 4. So 4x + 8 = 4(x + 2).

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

content.body: A circle is the set of all points equidistant from a fixed centre point. The distance from centre to any point on the circle is the radius (r). The diameter (d) passes through the centre: d = 2r. Circles appear everywhere in nature and engineering.

Circle formulae (π ≈ 3.14159 or 22/7):
Circumference (perimeter) = 2πr = πd
Area = πr²

Arc length = (θ/360°) × 2πr  (θ = angle in degrees)
Area of sector = (θ/360°) × πr²

content.title: Calculating with Circles
content.body:
Circle with radius = 7 cm:
Circumference = 2π × 7 = 44 cm (using π = 22/7)
Area = π × 7² = 22/7 × 49 = 154 cm²

Semicircle with radius = 5 cm:
Area = ½ × π × 5² = ½ × 78.54 = 39.27 cm²
Perimeter = πr + 2r = 15.71 + 10 = 25.71 cm

Parts of a circle:
Radius: centre to edge
Diameter: across the circle through centre
Chord: straight line joining two points on circle (not through centre)
Arc: part of the circumference
Sector: 'pie slice' region (two radii + arc)
Segment: region between a chord and an arc
Tangent: line touching circle at exactly one point

questions.question: Circle with r=10cm. Find circumference (π=3.14).
Circle with r=7cm. Find area (π=22/7).
Diameter = 20cm. What is the radius?
A line touching a circle at exactly one point is called:
questions.explanation: A tangent touches the circle at exactly one point and is perpendicular to the radius at that point.

questions.question: A sector has angle 90° and radius 6cm. Find its area (π≈3.14).
Circle with diameter 14cm. Find area (π=22/7).
A chord is:
options: A line joining two points on the circle
questions.answer: A line joining two points on the circle
questions.explanation: A chord joins two points on the circumference but does not pass through the centre (unless it's the diameter).

questions.question: A sector with angle 60° from a circle r=6cm. Find arc length (π≈3.14).
Which formula gives the area of a circle?
questions.explanation: Area of a circle = π × r² (radius squared times pi).

questions.question: A semicircle has diameter 12cm. Find its area (π≈3.14).
questions.explanation: r=6. Area of semicircle = ½πr² = ½×3.14×36 = 56.52 cm².

options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form

questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Geometry & Measurement. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Geometry & Measurement concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of mathematics.
Core Theory

This section of Geometry & Measurement builds on foundational knowledge. In mathematics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Geometry & Measurement. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Geometry & Measurement. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Geometry & Measurement include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Geometry & Measurement. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What does simplifying an expression mean?
options: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.answer: Reducing it to its simplest equivalent form
questions.explanation: Simplification means rewriting an expression in its simplest equivalent form by collecting like terms and applying arithmetic.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a variable?
questions.explanation: A variable is a letter (like x, y, n) that represents an unknown or changing value.

questions.question: What is 2x + 3x simplified?
questions.explanation: Collect like terms: 2x + 3x = 5x (both terms have the same variable x).

questions.question: Solve for x: x + 7 = 15
questions.explanation: Subtract 7 from both sides: x = 15 - 7 = 8.

questions.question: What is the result of expanding 4(y - 3)?
questions.explanation: Distribute: 4 × y - 4 × 3 = 4y - 12.

questions.question: Which operation reverses addition?
questions.explanation: Subtraction is the inverse (reverse) of addition.

questions.question: What is 3² equal to?
questions.explanation: 3² = 3 × 3 = 9. The exponent 2 means multiply the base by itself.

questions.question: Factorise: 4x + 8
questions.explanation: The HCF of 4x and 8 is 4. So 4x + 8 = 4(x + 2).

questions.question: What is the y-intercept of y = 3x + 5?
questions.explanation: In y = mx + c, c is the y-intercept. Here c = 5.

questions.question: Solve: 3x = 18
questions.explanation: Divide both sides by 3: x = 18 ÷ 3 = 6.

questions.question: What is the formula for the area of a rectangle?
questions.explanation: Area of a rectangle = length × width.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a like term with 3x²?
questions.explanation: Like terms must have the same variable AND the same power. 3x has power 1, not 2, so it is not a like term with 3x².

questions.question: What is the gradient/slope of a horizontal line?
questions.explanation: A horizontal line has no rise, so gradient = rise/run = 0/run = 0.

questions.question: If a = 4 and b = 3, find 2a + b.
questions.explanation: Substitute: 2(4) + 3 = 8 + 3 = 11.

questions.question: What does the inequality x > 5 mean?
questions.explanation: The > symbol means 'greater than'. x > 5 means x takes values greater than (but not equal to) 5.

questions.question: Solve: 2(x + 3) = 14
questions.explanation: Expand: 2x + 6 = 14. Subtract 6: 2x = 8. Divide: x = 4.

questions.question: The HCF of 12 and 18 is:
questions.explanation: Factors of 12: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Factors of 18: 1,2,3,6,9,18. Highest common factor = 6.

questions.question: What is the LCM of 4 and 6?
questions.explanation: Multiples of 4: 4,8,12,... Multiples of 6: 6,12,... Lowest common multiple = 12.

questions.question: Which number is prime?
questions.explanation: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. 7 is prime (factors: 1 and 7 only).

questions.question: What is 0.5 as a fraction?
